nativeRange.habitat: Sluggish rivers, streams, and their floodplains with sandy or muddy substrates, leaf litter, rocks, and submerged wood; often in areas with moderate current

decorPreferences: fine sand or smooth-gravel substrate for burrowing and foraging
driftwood and twisted roots for shelter and territory demarcation
leaf litter (Indian almond leaves) to mimic biotope
dense background and midground planting (tough-rooted species recommended)
subdued or dappled lighting with floating cover

diet.notes: Opportunistic omnivore with a strong preference for small invertebrates and snails. Eagerly consumes live and frozen meaty foods: bloodworms, tubifex, brine shrimp, daphnia, and chopped earthworms. Accepts quality sinking pellets, wafers, and bottom-feeder tablets. Snail-eating ability is well-documented — an effective biological control for pest snail populations (Physa, Melanoides, Bladder snails). Vegetable matter (blanched zucchini, cucumber, spinach, algae wafers) should be offered regularly. Will raid other fish’ food at the bottom; targeted feeding ensures adequate nutrition in mixed communities.

goodWith: medium-to-large tetras (black skirt, diamond, Congo tetra)
barbs (tiger barbs, rosy barbs, odessa barbs)
corydoras catfish (with caution — similar bottom zone)
medium cichlids that tolerate active tankmates
avoidWith: dwarf shrimp and small ornamental shrimp (will be eaten)
snails kept deliberately as ornamental specimens
other loach species without ample space and hiding spots

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase primary species image for Botia lohachata; shows characteristic reticulated body pattern. Verify availability before use.
Widely used reference image of adult Botia lohachata showing reticulate pattern; good detail and colour.

summary: The Reticulate Loach (Botia lohachata) is an active, social bottom-dweller from India and Pakistan, reaching ~12 cm. Juveniles display a striking Y-O-Y-O reticulated pattern that gives rise to the popular name “Yo-Yo Loach.” Best kept in shoals of 5+ in spacious, well-decorated tanks; a useful snail controller.
careNotes:
The Reticulate Loach is a lively, inquisitive loach from the river systems of northern India, Pakistan, and Nepal, typically sold under the trade name “Yo-Yo Loach.” The name comes from the juvenile reticulated dark-and-light body pattern that, when reading markings in sequence, loosely resembles the letters Y-O-Y-O. Adult patterning tends to become more subdued and complex with age.

Taxonomic note: Botia lohachata (Chaudhuri, 1912) describes the Indian population. The closely related Pakistani population has at various times been treated as the same species or as Botia almorhae Gray, 1831; SeriouslyFish and FishBase recognise these as distinct species. Aquarists should be aware that fish sold as “Yo-Yo Loach” may originate from either population, which are nearly identical in appearance and care requirements.

Shoaling is essential: lone or paired specimens become withdrawn, stressed, and may develop inter-species aggression; groups of 5 or more distribute social interactions and allow natural hierarchical behaviour. The species is active and will explore every level of the tank despite being a bottom-dweller.

A minimum tank length of 120 cm (150 L) is recommended for a shoal of five; larger groups benefit from 200 L or more. Fine sand or smooth substrate is preferred as the loach forages with its mouth pressed to the bottom. Sharp gravel risks injuring the sensitive barbels. Provide plentiful hiding spots (caves, driftwood crevices) to reduce stress and establish territories. Plants with robust root systems are preferred as the fish rearranges substrate during foraging.

The species is a reliable and enthusiastic consumer of pest snails (Physa, Bladder snail, Melanoides). It should not be kept with ornamental shrimp, which will be predated.

Water quality should be well-maintained with regular partial changes; the species is sensitive to ammonia and nitrite spikes. Like many loaches, it is susceptible to ich (white spot) and responds well to standard treatments at reduced doses if the fish is scaleless-adjacent — however Botia lohachata does possess small embedded scales and is not as copper-sensitive as true scaleless catfish.

The sub-orbital spine (a sharp erectile spine beneath the eye common to all Botia) can catch in fine-mesh nets; use a solid container when transferring fish.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is rarely achieved and not well-documented for home aquariums. Commercial specimens are typically wild-caught or farm-raised in large ponds in Asia using hormone induction. No reliable account of successful captive spawning in a home aquarium exists in mainstream hobbyist literature. Classified as “not in captivity” for practical purposes.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Botia lohachata
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~12 cm), temperature range, pH, hardness, native range (Ganges and Brahmaputra drainages, India), biotope, diet preferences, compatibility, breeding notes, and taxonomic discussion distinguishing B. lohachata from B. almorhae.

additional.name: FishBase — Botia lohachata
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 11.5 cm TL), temperature (24–30°C), taxonomy (Botiidae, Cypriniformes), and native range (India, Pakistan, Nepal). Diet listed as worms, small crustaceans, insects, and plant matter consistent with omnivore classification.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Yo-Yo Loach Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade husbandry guide. Confirmed shoaling requirement (groups of 5+), snail-eating utility, tank size recommendation (~40 gal / 150 L for a small group), diet (sinking pellets, frozen foods, snails), and temperament as semi-aggressive within species but peaceful toward larger community fish. Temperature 75–86°F (24–30°C) and pH 6.5–7.5 cited.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Yo-Yo Loach profile
additional.notes: UK trade profile. Confirmed adult size 12–15 cm in aquarium (using 15 cm upper bound for wild maximum), hardness 5–15 dGH, breeding “not bred in captivity,” group requirement of 5+, and sub-orbital spine caution. Noted pattern change from juvenile Y-O-Y-O lettering to adult reticulate marbling.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable captive spawning records for home aquariums. Commercial production relies on hormone-induced spawning in large outdoor ponds. Hobbyist breeding is not achievable under standard aquarium conditions.
